fuzzy_prd: We need a two-factor authentication engine that a PHP application can use to add a time-based one-time password second factor. The core capability is taking a shared secret and a point in time and generating a short numeric code; the server and the user's authenticator app must agree on the same code at the same moment. The engine should support verifying a user-submitted code with a configurable tolerance window to absorb small clock differences between the user's device and the server — importantly, the tolerance window only looks backward in time, not forward. Once a code has been accepted, it must be remembered so a second attempt with the same code at the same moment is refused even though the code is still time-valid. The engine should also produce the standard provisioning URI that authenticator apps import by scanning a QR code, and it should be able to present the raw secret as well as a grouped version with spaces every four characters for manual entry. Finally, there should be a recovery-code system: generate a batch of single-use alphanumeric codes of a configurable length, and allow spending them one at a time (a code that is already spent or does not exist reports failure rather than throwing an error). All outputs must be predictable machine-readable strings, not localized messages or exception dumps.

oracle_data.golden_api_signature: function generateCode(secret: string, algorithm: string, digits: int, seconds: int, timestamp: int, offset: int): string; function validateCode(secret: string, algorithm: string, digits: int, seconds: int, window: int, checks: array): array; function provisioningUri(secret: string, label: string, algorithm: string, digits: int): string; function consumeRecovery(recoveryCodes: array, consume: array): array

trigger_keywords: how is the TOTP time step counter derived
what is the HMAC key for code generation
how does the offset parameter work
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The time-step counter = floor(timestamp / seconds) + offset. The HMAC is computed over this counter encoded as an 8-byte big-endian unsigned integer using the Base32-decoded secret as the key. Dynamic truncation extracts a 31-bit integer; it is reduced mod 10^digits and left-zero-padded to exactly 'digits' characters. The output is 'code=<digits-char-string>'.

trigger_keywords: backward window only or forward too
which time steps are checked in validation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The validation window only looks backward: a code is accepted if it matches the code for the step containing 'at', or any of the 'window' steps immediately preceding it. A code from a future step (offset +1 or later from the current step) is never accepted. Window=0 means only the exact current step.

trigger_keywords: exact query parameter order in provisioning URI
which parameters appear in the otpauth URL
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The provisioning URI format is: otpauth://totp/<percent-encoded-label>?issuer=<issuer>&label=<percent-encoded-label>&secret=<secret>&algorithm=<UPPER-ALGORITHM>&digits=<digits>. The issuer is the portion of the label before the first colon. Query parameter order is: issuer, label, secret, algorithm, digits. ':' encodes to '%3A', '@' encodes to '%40'.

trigger_keywords: how is secret split into groups
four character chunks for manual entry
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The grouped secret splits the raw Base32 secret into chunks of exactly 4 characters, joined by single spaces, with no trailing space after the last chunk. For example 'KS72XBTN5PEBGX2IWBMVW44LXHPAQ7L3' becomes 'KS72 XBTN 5PEB GX2I WBMV W44L XHPA Q7L3'.

trigger_keywords: what marks a recovery code as used
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each recovery entry has a 'code' value and a 'used_at' field (null = unused). Consuming a code marks the first matching unused entry. Output per consume attempt: 'consume=<value> success=<true|false>'. After all attempts: 'unused=<count>' then 'contains_unused=<true|false>'. A null or absent recovery_codes set has 0 unused entries.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: the standard provisioning URI that authenticator apps import by scanning a QR code
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The 'otpauth://totp/...' URI format defined by Google Authenticator. The label path segment and query values are percent-encoded per RFC 3986. The algorithm name is uppercased (e.g., 'sha256' becomes 'SHA256' in the URI).

context_pointers.vague_reference: once a code has been accepted, it must be remembered
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Within a single validate_code session (one adapter call with multiple checks in the 'checks' array), the engine maintains an in-memory set of already-accepted codes. Submitting the same code string a second time in the same session returns valid=false even if the timestamp is unchanged.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific information about that constraint. Please refer to the project PRD and the golden implementation under Laragear__TwoFactor/golden/src/ for the authoritative specification.

feature1_generate_code.json.description: Generate a TOTP code for an explicit moment in time with optional offset.

feature2_1_validate_window.json.description: Validate a code with backward tolerance window.

feature2_2_validate_single_use.json.description: Single-use replay protection: accepted code is refused on second submission.

code=716347 at=1577910600 valid=true
code=716347 at=1577910600 valid=false

feature4_1_secret_raw.json.description: Expose the shared secret as its plain string form.

feature4_2_secret_grouped.json.description: Render the shared secret as four-character chunks separated by spaces.

consume=AAA success=false
consume=ZZZ success=false
